summary: 42nd President of the United States (1993-2001) and Governor of Arkansas before that, a Rhodes Scholar known as the "Comeback Kid" who defeated incumbent George H.W. Bush in the 1992 election. His presidency combined a booming economy, NAFTA, welfare reform, and the mostly-repealed Glass-Steagall Act with the Monica Lewinsky scandal, which led to his 1998 impeachment (and subsequent Senate acquittal) and a controversial round of last-day pardons known as "Pardongate."

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Bill Clinton, a Rhodes Scholar, served as Governor of Arkansas before becoming the 42nd President of the United States, defeating incumbent George H.W. Bush in the 1992 election. He is frequently identified in giveaways by this rise from Arkansas governor to the presidency and by defining moments of his 1992 campaign.

clues.clue: Governor of Arkansas before becoming the 42nd U.S. President, defeating incumbent George H.W. Bush in the 1992 election
examples: name this Governor of Arkansas.
name this scandal involving last-minute commuted sentences by the 42nd president.

works.name: Governor of Arkansas
works.description: As the young "Boy Governor" of Arkansas, Clinton was advised by a group nicknamed the "Three Beards," and his approval rating plummeted after he enacted a highway-construction tax that disproportionately hit the heavier, older vehicles owned by the poor. Footage of Cuban refugees rioting at Fort Chaffee was used against him in an attack ad by Frank White, who defeated him for a two-year stint out of office. He also presided over the Damascus Accident at a Titan II missile site, was accused by David Hale of forcing him into a business loan, and had campaign debt paid off by James McDougal, with whom he partnered in a failed Ozarks real estate venture that developed into the Whitewater scandal.

clues.clue: Known as the "Boy Governor"; advised by a group nicknamed the "Three Beards"; his approval rating plummeted after a highway tax disproportionately hit the heavier, older vehicles of the poor
examples: This governor, who was advised by the Three Beards, saw his approval rate plummet because a tax he enacted to subsidize highway construction disproportionately taxed the heavier older vehicles of the poor.
This "Boy Governor" presided over the Damascus Accident at a Titan II missile site.

clues.clue: Footage of Cuban refugees rioting at Fort Chaffee was used against him in an attack ad by Frank White, who ousted him from the governorship for two years
examples: Clips of a group of Cuban refugees rioting in Fort Chaffee were used in an attack ad against this man by Frank White who turned him out of office for 2 years.

clues.clue: David Hale claimed the governor forced him to make a business loan
examples: After leaving office, David Hale claimed that this governor had forced him to make a business loan.

clues.clue: James McDougal paid off some of his campaign debt as a Rhodes Scholar and partnered with him, the "Comeback Kid," in a failed Ozarks real estate venture that developed into the Whitewater scandal
examples: Some of this Rhodes Scholar's debt following one campaign was paid off by James McDougal, who partnered with this "Comeback Kid" in a failed real estate venture in the Ozarks which developed into the Whitewater scandal.

works.name: The 1992 Presidential Campaign
works.description: Clinton's 1992 campaign against incumbent George H.W. Bush leaned on the slogan "it's the economy, stupid" and included a widely remembered appearance playing the saxophone on The Arsenio Hall Show.

clues.clue: Played the saxophone on The Arsenio Hall Show during his successful 1992 presidential campaign
examples: name this president who played the saxophone on The Arsenio Hall Show.
Another ploy to win younger voters was this Democrat's appearance playing the saxophone on The Arsenio Hall Show in a successful campaign to win the 1992 presidential election.

clues.clue: Used the slogan "it's the economy, stupid" in defeating George H.W. Bush
examples: The Glass-Steagall Act was repealed under this president, who used the slogan "it's the economy, stupid" in his re-election campaign and signed the North American Free Trade Agreement.

works.name: Domestic Policy and Legislation
works.description: Clinton's domestic agenda included signing NAFTA, a 1993 assault weapons ban that helped fuel the 1994 "Republican Revolution," and the largely repealed Glass-Steagall Act. After Republicans led by Newt Gingrich took Congress in 1994, he worked with them to pass welfare reform, having promised to "end welfare as we have come to know it," even as his own health care reform initiative -- which would have included a prescription drug benefit -- failed. He also signed the Defense of Marriage Act and the Omnibus Crime Bill, adopted the "Don't Ask, Don't Tell" policy, and spoke out against California's Proposition 187, comparing it to "bringing Big Brother into the schools."

clues.clue: Presided over the mostly repeal of the Glass-Steagall Act
examples: Glass-Steagall was mostly repealed under this president. This president signed the Defense of Marriage Act and the Omnibus Crime Bill and faced a scandal for an affair with Monica Lewisnky.
The Glass-Steagall Act was repealed under this president, who used the slogan "it's the economy, stupid" in his re-election campaign and signed the North American Free Trade Agreement.

clues.clue: Worked with House Republicans led by Newt Gingrich to pass welfare reform, having promised to "end welfare as we have come to know it"; his own health care reform initiative, which would have included a prescription drug benefit, failed
examples: This politician, who promised to "end welfare as we have come to know it," was called "our first black president" by Toni Morrison at the height of a scandal involving Paula Jones and Gennifer Flowers.
This president's failed attempt at health care reform would have included a prescription benefit. His administration worked with House Republicans led by Newt Gingrich to pass welfare reform.

clues.clue: Signed the Defense of Marriage Act and the Omnibus Crime Bill; his administration adopted the "Don't Ask, Don't Tell" policy
examples: Glass-Steagall was mostly repealed under this president. This president signed the Defense of Marriage Act and the Omnibus Crime Bill and faced a scandal for an affair with Monica Lewisnky.

This president spoke out against Proposition 187, comparing it to "bringing Big Brother into the schools." Under this president's administration, the Department of Defense adopted the "Don't Ask, Don't Tell" policy.

clues.clue: Signed a 1993 assault weapons ban that helped fuel the Republican Revolution of the 1994 midterms
examples: The Republican Revolution was partly driven by this president's passage of an assault weapons ban in 1993. This president's eight-year term began with the defeat of George H.W. Bush in the 1992 election.

clues.clue: Signed the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA)
examples: The Glass-Steagall Act was repealed under this president, who used the slogan "it's the economy, stupid" in his re-election campaign and signed the North American Free Trade Agreement.

clues.clue: Spoke out against California's Proposition 187, comparing it to "bringing Big Brother into the schools"
examples: This president spoke out against Proposition 187, comparing it to "bringing Big Brother into the schools."

clues.clue: Endured multiple government shutdowns after Newt Gingrich's Republicans won the 1994 midterm elections
examples: Gingrich began several government shutdowns during this president's term after Republicans emerged victorious from the 1994 midterm elections.

works.name: Race Relations
works.description: Clinton was advised on race issues by Vernon Jordan, the former president of the National Urban League, even as Jesse Jackson once called him "nothing but an appetite" in Joan Didion's essay "Clinton Agonistes." He was criticized by civil rights activists for rushing home from a primary campaign stop to supervise the execution of Ricky Ray Rector, and for a "Sister Souljah moment" in which he criticized the rapper's remarks on the Rodney King riots during a Rainbow Coalition speech. Despite the Lewinsky scandal, novelist Toni Morrison famously called him "our first black president."

clues.clue: Advised on race issues by Vernon Jordan, former president of the National Urban League; Jesse Jackson called him "nothing but an appetite" in Joan Didion's essay "Clinton Agonistes"
examples: A Joan Didion essay titled "[this politician] Agonistes" notes how Jesse Jackson once referred to him as "nothing but an appetite." This politician was advised on race issues by the former president of the National Urban League, Vernon Jordan.

clues.clue: Criticized by civil rights activists for rushing home from a primary campaign stop to supervise the execution of brain-damaged Ricky Ray Rector
examples: Civil rights activists criticized this person for rushing home from a primary to supervise the execution of a brain-damaged African-American man, Ricky Ray Rector.
Norm Macdonald later called this person a murderer on an episode of The View.

clues.clue: In a "Sister Souljah moment," criticized the rapper at a Rainbow Coalition speech over her remarks on the Rodney King riots
examples: In a speech to the Rainbow Coalition, this politician criticized the rapper Sister Souljah for her remarks on the Rodney King riots.

clues.clue: Called "our first black president" by novelist Toni Morrison, at the height of the Paula Jones and Gennifer Flowers scandals
examples: This politician, who promised to "end welfare as we have come to know it," was called "our first black president" by Toni Morrison at the height of a scandal involving Paula Jones and Gennifer Flowers.

clues.clue: Amused civil rights activist and comedian Dick Gregory by making Newt Gingrich sit in the back of Air Force One
examples: This president amused Gregory by making Newt Gingrich sit in the back of Air Force One.

works.name: Foreign Policy
works.description: Clinton's Cuba policy included the Helms-Burton Act, which codified the embargo after the Cuban exile group Brothers to the Rescue had two planes shot down, the arrest of the Wasp network's "Cuban Five" spies, and the "wet foot, dry foot" immigration policy adopted amid a Special Period immigration surge; his administration's raid returning Elian Gonzalez to Cuba, ordered through Attorney General Janet Reno, is credited with hurting Al Gore's 2000 Florida campaign. Elsewhere, businessman Mansoor Ijaz ran off-the-books negotiations with Sudan on Clinton's behalf, and after the 1999 NATO bombing of the Chinese Embassy in Belgrade during the Kosovo War, Chinese state media withheld broadcast of his official apology for four days.

clues.clue: The Helms-Burton Act codified the Cuban embargo after the exile group Brothers to the Rescue had two planes shot down
examples: During this man's presidency, the Cuban embargo was codified by the Helms-Burton Act after two planes operated by the Cuban exile group Brothers to the Rescue were shot down.

clues.clue: The "Cuban Five" spies from the Wasp network were arrested during his tenure
examples: Spies from the Wasp network known as the Cuban Five were arrested during this President's tenure.

clues.clue: Cuba's Special Period immigration surge prompted the "wet foot, dry foot" immigration policy
examples: During an immigration surge prompted by Cuba's Special Period, this President restricted immigration to those reaching US land under the wet foot, dry foot policy.

clues.clue: Ordered, through Attorney General Janet Reno, the raid that returned Elian Gonzalez to Cuba, seen as having hurt Al Gore's 2000 Florida campaign
examples: The raid that culminated in the return of Elian Gonzalez to Cuba was ordered by this president's attorney general Janet Reno.
name this President whose handling of the Gonzalez case is sometimes credited with hurting his vice president Al Gore's campaign in Florida.

clues.clue: Businessman Mansoor Ijaz ran off-the-books negotiations with Sudan on his behalf
examples: Mansoor Ijaz ran off-the-books negotiations with Sudan for this president.

clues.clue: After the 1999 bombing of the Chinese Embassy in Belgrade during the Kosovo War, Chinese state media withheld broadcast of his official apology for four days; Vice President Hu Jintao called the attack "barbaric"
examples: Chinese state media refused to broadcast the official apology of this US President for 4 days after the bombing incident occurred. During this time, Vice President Hu Jintao described the attack as "barbaric."

works.name: Impeachment and the Lewinsky Scandal
works.description: Clinton's affair with White House intern Monica Lewinsky, investigated by independent counsel Ken Starr (whose Starr Report went to Congress) alongside the earlier Paula Jones lawsuit, produced his famous grand jury hedge that "it depends on what the meaning of the word 'is' is." He was impeached by the House in 1998 on four counts including perjury and abuse of power -- during which prospective Speaker Bob Livingston resigned after information surfaced from Larry Flynt, and aide Cheryl Mills gained national attention defending him -- before being acquitted by the Senate.

clues.clue: In grand jury testimony, said "there is absolutely no sex of any kind" between himself and Monica Lewinsky, since "it depends on what the meaning of the word 'is' is"
examples: Name this President who, in grand jury testimony, agreed that "there is absolutely no sex of any kind" between himself and Monica Lewinsky since "it depends on what the meaning of the word 'is' is."

clues.clue: Independent counsel Ken Starr investigated the affair and delivered the Starr Report to Congress; the case included Paula Jones' deposition
examples: Maureen Dowd's column "Confessions of a Sex Addict" ... turned out to be about this independent counsel investigating Clinton, who delivered his namesake report to Congress in 1998.
Darrell Hammond's impersonations of this man include an SNL cold open where he reads Paula Jones' deposition before being dragged away by Kenneth Starr.

clues.clue: Impeached by the House in 1998 on four counts including perjury and abuse of power; prospective Speaker Bob Livingston resigned after information surfaced from Larry Flynt, and aide Cheryl Mills gained attention defending him
examples: During this event, prospective Speaker of the House Bob Livingston resigned his seat due to information unearthed by Larry Flynt.

Cheryl Mills first gained national attention as part of this event, in which she said "I'm not worried about civil rights." Four counts including perjury and abuse of power formed the charges in this event.

works.name: Bill Clinton's Pardons Scandal ("Pardongate")
works.description: On his last day in office, Clinton issued a controversial round of pardons and commuted sentences that became known as "Pardongate." Beneficiaries included commodities trader Marc Rich, founder of Glencore, who had donated over $400,000 to Clinton's library fund, and Clinton's own half-brother Roger; the scandal was investigated by U.S. attorneys Mary Jo White and James Comey and also helped former Navajo tribal chairman Peter MacDonald and enabled lawyer Harvey Weinig, who had funneled money for the Cali drug cartel.

clues.clue: Beneficiary Marc Rich, founder of Glencore, had donated over $400,000 to Clinton's presidential library fund; the scandal also helped his half-brother Roger
examples: One beneficiary of this scandal had earlier founded Glencore and gave over four hundred thousand dollars to the sitting president's library fund.
This scandal helped out people like the president's half-brother Roger and hedge fund manager Marc Rich.

clues.clue: Investigated by U.S. attorneys Mary Jo White and James Comey
examples: Mary Jo White and James Comey were both appointed to investigate this scandal.

clues.clue: Helped former Navajo tribal chairman Peter MacDonald and lawyer Harvey Weinig, who had funneled money for the Cali drug cartel
examples: A former Navajo tribal chairman named Peter MacDonald was helped by this scandal.
One man involved in this scandal was a lawyer named Harvey Weinig who funneled money for the Cali drug cartel.

clues.clue: Defended in his memoir My Life; also involved former Congressman Dan Rostenkowski
examples: A defense of this scandal, which involved people like former Congressman Dan Rostenkowski, was offered in the memoir, My Life.

works.name: Legacy and Cultural Depictions
works.description: Clinton has been a fixture of American comedy: Phil Hartman played him in an SNL sketch stealing food from customers at a McDonald's, and he has been the subject of bits by Bill Burr and Norm Macdonald. He met the mother of comedian John Mulaney while escorting female students home from the library at Georgetown University. His lengthy 2004 autobiography, My Life, was met with a rebuttal book, Because He Could, from former advisor Dick Morris.

clues.clue: Phil Hartman played him in an SNL sketch stealing food from customers at a McDonald's
examples: In a Saturday Night Live sketch, Phil Hartman depicted him stealing food from customers at a McDonald's.

clues.clue: The subject of comedy bits by Bill Burr ("soft hands") and Norm Macdonald (a joke about using medical marijuana to deliver a speech on crutches)
examples: This person has "soft hands" according to Bill Burr, whose friend missed seeing this person after going to get a slice of pizza.
Norm Macdonald joked that this person used medical marijuana in order to deliver a speech on crutches.

clues.clue: Escorted female students home from the library at Georgetown University, where he met the mother of comedian John Mulaney
examples: This person would escort female students home from the library at Georgetown, where he met the mother of John Mulaney.

clues.clue: Wrote the lengthy 2004 autobiography My Life; former advisor Dick Morris wrote the rebuttal book Because He Could
examples: This man's lengthy autobiography was called My Life. His former advisor, Dick Morris, wrote the rebuttal book Because He Could.

comprehensive_summary: Bill Clinton, a Rhodes Scholar, first rose to prominence as the young "Boy Governor" of Arkansas, advised by a group nicknamed the "Three Beards"; a highway tax that hit older, poorer drivers' vehicles tanked his approval and, combined with an attack ad using footage of rioting Cuban refugees at Fort Chaffee, cost him reelection to Frank White for two years. As governor he was accused by David Hale of forcing a business loan, and campaign debt paid off by James McDougal led to a failed Ozarks real estate venture that became the Whitewater scandal. Dubbed the "Comeback Kid," he won the presidency in 1992 over incumbent George H.W. Bush on the strength of the slogan "it's the economy, stupid" and a memorable saxophone performance on The Arsenio Hall Show. As the 42nd president, he signed NAFTA, a 1993 assault weapons ban that helped spark the 1994 Republican Revolution, and worked with Newt Gingrich's Republicans on welfare reform after promising to "end welfare as we have come to know it," even as his own health care reform initiative failed; he also signed the Defense of Marriage Act and Omnibus Crime Bill, adopted "Don't Ask, Don't Tell," opposed California's Proposition 187, endured government shutdowns, and presided over the largely repeal of the Glass-Steagall Act. On race, he was advised by Vernon Jordan even as Jesse Jackson dismissed him as "nothing but an appetite"; he was criticized for supervising the execution of Ricky Ray Rector and for a "Sister Souljah moment" at a Rainbow Coalition speech, yet was later called "our first black president" by Toni Morrison. His foreign policy included a hardline Cuba stance (the Helms-Burton Act, the Cuban Five arrests, the "wet foot, dry foot" policy, and the Elian Gonzalez raid that reportedly hurt Al Gore's 2000 campaign), back-channel Sudan negotiations via Mansoor Ijaz, and the fallout from the 1999 bombing of the Chinese Embassy in Belgrade during the Kosovo War. His affair with Monica Lewinsky, investigated by independent counsel Ken Starr, produced his notorious "meaning of the word 'is'" testimony and led to his 1998 impeachment on perjury and abuse-of-power charges -- during which Bob Livingston resigned his bid for Speaker -- before his Senate acquittal. His last day in office brought a controversial wave of pardons ("Pardongate") benefiting Marc Rich, his half-brother Roger, Peter MacDonald, and others, investigated by Mary Jo White and James Comey. He remains a fixture of American popular culture, from Phil Hartman's and Darrell Hammond's SNL portrayals to bits by Bill Burr and Norm Macdonald, and wrote the memoir My Life, rebutted by Dick Morris's Because He Could.
recursive_suggestions: Hillary Clinton -- Bill Clinton's wife and Secretary of State; distinct History figure with her own extensive question presence
Monica Lewinsky -- central figure in the impeachment scandal; potentially independent topic
Kenneth Starr -- independent counsel who investigated the Lewinsky scandal
Newt Gingrich -- Speaker of the House during the Republican Revolution and welfare reform negotiations
Marc Rich -- central figure in the pardons scandal; founder of Glencore
George H.W. Bush -- defeated by Clinton in 1992; distinct History figure
Elian Gonzalez -- could support a standalone Cuba-U.S. relations topic
Whitewater scandal -- could be treated as its own dedicated topic distinct from Clinton's biography
